attrs.transmission: Contact with humans, animals, soil; infected skin scales/hair
attrs.pathogenesis: Penetrate stratum corneum → proliferate in keratinized layers; secreted proteases; can invade hair shaft (endothrix/ectothrix) and nail
attrs.disease: Tinea / ringworm (capitis, pedis, corporis, cruris, unguium)
attrs.diagnosis: KOH + calcofluor white; culture (Sabouraud) if unresponsive
attrs.treatment: Topical terbinafine/azoles; extensive → griseofulvin, itraconazole, terbinafine
attrs.special: Least invasive — colonize only keratinized tissue; course depends on site, immune response, growth/peeling dynamics

attrs.pathogenesis: Lipophilic yeast overgrows on sebum-rich skin
attrs.disease: Pityriasis (tinea) versicolor — hypo/hyperpigmented scaling patches on trunk
attrs.virulence: Lipophilic; budding yeast cells mixed with short hyphae ("spaghetti and meatballs")
attrs.diagnosis: KOH (yeast + hyphae); grows only on lipid-enriched media

attrs.pathogenesis: Melanized fungus, limited skin peeling, unaltered skin architecture
attrs.disease: Tinea nigra — macular pigmented lesions on palms/soles
attrs.special: Distinguished from melanoma by limited inflammation

attrs.pathogenesis: Conidia inoculated into wound → slow progressive spread along lymphatics
attrs.disease: Sporotrichosis — ulcerating nodules with lymphatic (lymphocutaneous) spread
attrs.virulence: 37 °C yeast / 25 °C mold (mold = infectious form in nature)
attrs.treatment: Oral potassium iodide; itraconazole/terbinafine; amphotericin B if systemic

attrs.virulence: Pigmented (melanized) molds; muriform bodies in tissue
attrs.diagnosis: Histology — brown muriform bodies, septate hyphae; pigmented mold culture

attrs.pathogenesis: Chronic granules of mycelium embedded in cementing substance
attrs.disease: Mycetoma (Madura foot) — chronic, disfiguring; can involve bone
attrs.virulence: Mycelium embedded in CaCO₃ cementing granules
attrs.diagnosis: Serology complex (many agents); grains in discharge
attrs.special: Hallmark: fistulous tracts draining pus + solid grains/granules

attrs.transmission: Endogenous (oral, GIT, female genital flora); rarely direct contact
attrs.pathogenesis: Mannoprotein adhesion to fibronectin; hyphal transition → tissue invasion, biofilm, secreted aspartic proteases (SAP)
attrs.disease: Thrush (oral), vulvovaginal candidiasis; invasive candidiasis (catheters, immunocompromised)
attrs.keyVF: Mannoprotein adhesins; SAP proteases; biofilm; yeast↔hyphal dimorphism
attrs.virulence: SAP digest collagen → deep invasion; biofilms on catheters resist drugs/immunity
attrs.diagnosis: KOH (yeast + pseudohyphae); blood/tissue culture for invasive
attrs.treatment: Nystatin / topical azoles (superficial); fluconazole, echinocandins, amphotericin B (invasive)
attrs.special: Predisposing: antibiotics, immunosuppression, diabetes (↑ mannoproteins), catheters

attrs.pathogenesis: Branching SEPTATE hyphae (acute-angle); invades lung tissue in immunocompromised
attrs.disease: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis; aspergilloma (fungus ball); allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis
attrs.virulence: Septate hyphae with acute-angle branching; invades vessels → necrosis/dissemination
attrs.diagnosis: Pulmonary aspirate/BAL/biopsy; galactomannan antigen; molecular markers
attrs.treatment: Voriconazole / amphotericin B; surgical removal of localized lesions
attrs.special: Invasive disease mainly in severe immunosuppression; aspergilloma colonizes pre-existing cavities

attrs.virulence: Invades blood vessels → thrombosis, tissue necrosis, cranial nerve palsy
attrs.treatment: High-dose amphotericin B; control underlying disease; surgical debridement
attrs.special: Rhino-cerebral form can be rapidly fatal; classic in diabetic ketoacidosis

attrs.pathogenesis: Low-virulence; proliferates in alveoli of immunocompromised
attrs.disease: Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) — dyspnea, dry non-productive cough, alveolar infiltrates (esp. AIDS)
attrs.virulence: Alveolar proliferation; disease tied to immune status (low CD4)
attrs.diagnosis: BAL with special stains; antigen detection; molecular markers
attrs.special: Atypical fungus, NON-cultivable; classic AIDS-defining pneumonia

attrs.transmission: Inhalation; soil contaminated with bird (pigeon) droppings
attrs.pathogenesis: Polysaccharide capsule; survives in macrophages (melanin/laccase); high CNS affinity
attrs.disease: Cryptococcal meningitis (esp. AIDS); pulmonary; skin/bone if disseminated
attrs.virulence: Capsule is antiphagocytic & immunosuppressive; laccase makes melanin → resists oxidative killing; crosses BBB in macrophages
attrs.diagnosis: India-ink (capsule); capsular antigen test (quantitative); CSF culture (large volume)
attrs.treatment: Amphotericin B + flucytosine → consolidation with fluconazole
attrs.special: Most common systemic fungal infection in AIDS; encapsulated yeast (not dimorphic)

attrs.transmission: Inhalation of conidia; soil/caves with bird or BAT droppings (no person-to-person)
attrs.pathogenesis: Conidia → yeast in lungs; intracellular growth in macrophages; spread to RES
attrs.disease: Histoplasmosis — usually mild/calcified nodules; TB-like pulmonary; disseminated (RES, skin, GIT) in immunocompromised
attrs.diagnosis: Antigen (polysaccharide) in blood/urine; culture (slow); serology cross-reacts
attrs.special: Associated with bird/bat droppings; can be localized OR disseminated

attrs.transmission: Inhalation of conidia (overlaps Histoplasma areas; not bird/bat-linked)
attrs.disease: Blastomycosis — pulmonary; skin lesions (most common dissemination), bone, GU/prostate
attrs.diagnosis: KOH — large yeasts with broad-based buds; H&E; molecular/serology

attrs.transmission: Inhalation of arthroconidia; alkaline soil of semi-arid climates (no person-to-person)
attrs.disease: Coccidioidomycosis ("Valley fever") — mostly self-limiting; chronic pulmonary; dissemination (bone, skin, CNS meningitis) rare
attrs.virulence: Highly infectious arthroconidia; unique spherule containing endospores
attrs.diagnosis: KOH/calcofluor — large spherules; serology (IgM early, IgG later)
attrs.treatment: Self-limiting often; itraconazole/fluconazole; amphotericin B (disseminated); fluconazole for meningitis
attrs.special: Tissue form = spherule; ~90% asymptomatic; semi-arid soil

attrs.disease: Paracoccidioidomycosis (South American blastomycosis) — chronic mucocutaneous ulcers, RES & lung involvement
attrs.virulence: Yeast cells with multiple peripheral budding blastoconidia
attrs.diagnosis: Direct — "captain’s wheel" budding yeast; culture; serology
attrs.special: Far more frequent in males — estrogens inhibit mold→yeast conversion

questions.stem: The metabolism of fungi is of which type?
questions.explanation: Fungi are chemoheterotrophs — they use organic molecules as both carbon and energy source.

questions.stem: What distinguishes fungi from other eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Fungi have a chitin cell wall, ergosterol (not cholesterol) in the membrane, and are haploid (except transient diploid in sexual reproduction). They ARE true eukaryotes (have a nucleus).

questions.stem: Which molecules in the fungal cell wall induce an immune response in the human host?

options.t: All of the above (mannans/mannoproteins, glucans, chitin)

questions.explanation: Mannans/mannoproteins, glucans and chitin can all trigger host immune responses.

questions.stem: Dimorphism of fungi:

options.t: Can be induced artificially in the laboratory

questions.explanation: Dimorphism (temperature-dependent yeast↔mold switch) is inducible in vitro and is important for invasiveness/pathogenesis.

questions.stem: Which terms are associated with the SEXUAL reproduction of fungi?
questions.explanation: Sexual = teleomorph, producing spores via meiosis. Conidia and the anamorph form are asexual.

questions.stem: Which terms are associated with the ASEXUAL reproduction of fungi?
questions.explanation: Asexual = anamorph, producing conidia / budding. Teleomorph is the sexual form.

questions.stem: What types of conidia arise directly from fragmented hyphae?
questions.explanation: Arthroconidia form by fragmentation of hyphae (e.g. Coccidioides).

questions.stem: Polyene antifungal agents have which mechanism of action?

options.t: They form pores in the plasma membrane

questions.explanation: Polyenes (amphotericin B, nystatin) bind ergosterol and form membrane pores → loss of membrane integrity.

questions.stem: Which classes of antifungal agents target the biosynthetic pathway of ergosterol?
questions.explanation: Azoles (inhibit 14-α-demethylase) and allylamines (inhibit squalene epoxidase) block ergosterol SYNTHESIS. Polyenes BIND ergosterol; echinocandins target the cell wall.

questions.stem: Which antifungal class targets the fungal cell wall (β-1,3-glucan synthesis)?
questions.explanation: Echinocandins inhibit β-1,3-D-glucan synthase → cell-wall instability.

questions.stem: Compared with bacteria, the resistance mechanisms of fungi:

options.t: Do NOT include the production of drug-degrading hydrolytic enzymes
Are identical to those of bacteria

questions.explanation: Unlike bacteria (e.g. β-lactamases), fungi do not resist by secreting drug-degrading hydrolytic enzymes; they use target alteration, efflux, bypass.

questions.stem: What is the treatment of a clinical sample with KOH intended to do?

options.t: Degrade the host tissue present in the sample (leaving the fungal wall)
Cause swelling of the fungal cells

questions.explanation: KOH digests host tissue/keratin but not the chitin-rich fungal wall, revealing fungal elements.

questions.stem: Which type of fungi spread via the lymphatic route?
questions.explanation: Subcutaneous fungi (e.g. Sporothrix) spread via subcutaneous/lymphatic routes from the inoculation site.

questions.stem: Infections caused by subcutaneous fungi are acquired through:
questions.explanation: Subcutaneous mycoses are acquired by traumatic inoculation through the skin.

questions.stem: What is the most characteristic feature of mycetoma?

options.t: Formation of fistulous tracts that drain pus and solid grains/granules
Bone deformity in the initial stages
Treatment is exclusively surgical and always curative

questions.explanation: Mycetoma is characterized by draining fistulous tracts discharging grains (granules of mycelium in cementing substance).

questions.stem: Which statement represents a typical characteristic of systemic pathogenic fungi?

options.t: They are caused mainly by dimorphic fungi
They are transmitted from person to person
They are present in the microbiota of a healthy host
They cause infection exclusively in immunocompromised individuals

questions.explanation: Systemic mycoses are typically caused by dimorphic fungi (mold in environment, yeast in host); they are NOT commensals, are not person-to-person, and can infect healthy hosts.

questions.stem: Dermatophytes:

options.t: Are part of the gut microbiota

questions.explanation: Dermatophytes are the least invasive fungi — confined to keratinized tissue (skin, hair, nails).

questions.stem: Which infectious agent is commonly associated with pneumonia in immunocompromised individuals (fungal)?
questions.explanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii causes PCP, a classic AIDS-defining pneumonia.

questions.stem: Which fungus is the most common cause of the oral infection known as "thrush"?
questions.explanation: Oral thrush is caused by Candida albicans.

questions.stem: Which fungus is the most common cause of vaginal (yeast) infections?
questions.explanation: Vulvovaginal candidiasis is caused by Candida albicans.

questions.stem: Infections caused by Histoplasma capsulatum:

options.t: Are associated with environments contaminated by bird droppings
Can appear in a localized or a disseminated form
Are reliably diagnosed by a trial KOH treatment

questions.explanation: Histoplasma is linked to bird/bat-dropping-contaminated soil and causes localized or disseminated disease; KOH is of low yield.

questions.stem: Which statement about infections caused by Coccidioides sp. is NOT correct?

options.t: They cause symptoms in the entire infected population
They have a unique tissue form called a spherule
They are found mainly in semi-arid climates

questions.explanation: ~90% of Coccidioides infections are asymptomatic — so "symptoms in the entire population" is the incorrect statement. The others are true (spherule, semi-arid soil, self-limiting primary disease).

questions.stem: The pathogenic species of the genus Candida:

options.t: Adhere to the fibronectin of epithelial cells (via mannoproteins)
Are transmitted mainly by mosquito vectors

questions.explanation: Candida adheres to host fibronectin through mannoproteins, then invades using secreted proteases.

questions.stem: Aspergillosis can present with which clinical manifestations?
questions.explanation: Aspergillus → invasive pneumonia, aspergilloma, and allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. Lymphocutaneous spread is sporotrichosis.

questions.stem: Which of the following are dimorphic fungi?
questions.explanation: Histoplasma, Coccidioides, Blastomyces, Paracoccidioides, Sporothrix (and Candida) are dimorphic; Aspergillus is a mold.
